text: It is somewhat comforting that now, whenever I see a post about “here’s the thing that keeps me up at night” I know that there is absolutely no chance that this is being written by a human who is staying up all night.

OpenClaw starts out very fun, then the headaches start happening after a few weeks. 

I’ve talked to many AI agent power users who are also struggling with OpenClaw. 

Gateway issues, cronjobs not firing, skills not being used consistently, memory fall off, integrations need

I tested the highest-performing AI coding workflow of 2026.

It doesn't use one model. It uses two competing models against each other.

Opus 4.7 plans. GPT-5.5 executes.

The results aren't close.

(Prompts included) https://t.co/1CWrHmi9k0

Here's the problem with single-model workflows.

Planning and executing are two completely different cognitive tasks. Asking one model to do both is like hiring the same person as your strategist and your builder.

Some models think beautifully but execute loosely. 

Others

Dan Shipper at Every tested this on their Senior Engineer Benchmark.

The scores:
Opus 4.7 alone: low 30s GPT-5.5 alone: low-to-mid 40s Opus 4.7 planning + GPT-5.5 executing: 62.5

For reference, human senior engineers score 80-90.

The combo nearly doubled either model's

Why this works.

Opus 4.7 writes tight, contract-style plans. Exact file counts. Line limits. First-principles architecture. 

It thinks like a senior engineer scoping a project.

GPT-5.5 reads that plan and executes with confidence. 

It deletes files, rewrites from scratch,

The exact workflow:

Step 1: Open Claude. Select Opus 4.7.

Prompt:
"Analyze this codebase. Write a detailed rewrite plan from first principles. Include exact file structure, line limits per file, and architectural decisions. Do not write any code. Plan only."

Step 2: Copy the full plan Opus generates.

Open ChatGPT Codex. Select GPT-5.5.

Prompt:
"Here is a plan written by a senior engineer for rewriting this codebase from first principles. Execute it exactly as specified. Do not deviate from the plan. If something is ambiguous, make

Step 3: Let GPT-5.5 run.

It will work through the plan methodically. 

The key insight: GPT-5.5 is more confident when given explicit instructions from an external plan than when it generates its own.

It stops second-guessing. It stops being lazy. It builds.

This works beyond coding.

Content strategy: use Opus to outline the argument structure. Use GPT-5.5 to draft.

Research: use Opus to design the research methodology. Use GPT-5.5 to execute the analysis.

Business planning: use Opus to define the framework. 

Use GPT-5.5 to

The models swapped personalities this month.

Opus 4.7 got more rigid and precise. GPT-5.5 got more natural and conversational.

That's not a bug. That's why this combo works. 

One model became the perfect planner. The other became the perfect executor.

Use them for what

Stop asking one AI to do everything.

The frontier isn't one model. 
It's knowing which model to use for which job.

If you’re still paying premium prices to test “agentic coding,” you’re already late.

API + Chat, OpenAI/Anthropic protocol support, and built to plug into real dev workflows.

Tutorial: How to set up Codex Pet that looks like you 

Codex Pets are cute animated desktop companions in Codex that show what your AI coding agent is doing, like running, waiting for input, or ready for review. 

Simple workflow how to make one 👇 https://t.co/hjGl5qupXf

Step 1: 
Enter this line in your Codex app: $ skill-installer hatch-pet

(no space after $) 

Restart Codex https://t.co/y1WwfsY7Rv

Step 2: 
Run $ hatch-pet [description of your pet]

(no space after $) https://t.co/cffTy4snzu

Step 3: 
Use a photo of you or your profile photo (like I used) to generate a pet that looks like you https://t.co/ZG6HC6dmd6

Step 4: 
Wait, when it's finished, go to Codex Settings Appearance -> Pets and choose your personal pet from the list (usually at the bottom) https://t.co/tgkOwKiP89

Step 5:
When you need to wake up your pet write /pet in your prompt line. It'll follow you to every app, and when you code it'll show you what's going on. You can also add "reply" when you get any ideas what to add. https://t.co/CmZePyzUVj

I don't know if this is a skill issue or something... But I've been playing with OpenClaw a ton for the past 4ish months. I feel like lately I'm spending more time troubleshooting issues with it and telling it what it's doing wrong than I am actually getting valuable use from it.
